questions.question: Explain the concept of path compression in Patricia trees and how it differs from standard tries.
keyPoints: Path compression merges nodes with single children into single edges
Reduces the total number of nodes in the tree
Standard tries store one character per node; Patricia trees store variable-length strings per edge
Results in space optimization while maintaining O(k) search complexity

questions.question: What are the four types of nodes in Ethereum's Merkle Patricia Tree? Explain each briefly.
keyPoints: Empty node: Represents no data, used for non-existent paths
Leaf node: Contains the end of a path and stores an actual value
Extension node: Shares a common prefix with its child to compress the tree
Branch node: Has 16 children (for hex characters) and optionally stores a value

questions.question: Why is the Merkle Patricia Tree preferred over simple Merkle trees for blockchain state storage?
keyPoints: Supports efficient key-value lookups (not just proof generation)
Allows efficient insertion, deletion, and updates of state data
Provides cryptographic integrity verification through root hashing
Space-efficient due to Patricia compression
Each block maintains state snapshot via root hash

questions.question: Describe the time and space complexity of operations in a Patricia tree.
keyPoints: Search: O(k) where k is key length
Insert: O(k) time complexity
Delete: O(k) time complexity
Space: More efficient than tries due to path compression, approximately O(n×k) worst case but typically much less
Space savings depend on prefix sharing in the dataset

questions.question: How does changing a single value in the Merkle Patricia Tree affect the root hash? Explain the cryptographic significance.
keyPoints: Any change in the tree causes the root hash to change completely
This is due to the avalanche effect in cryptographic hash functions
The new root hash can be computed by updating hashes along the path from changed node to root
Provides tamper detection - any unauthorized change invalidates the root hash
Allows lightweight clients to verify state without downloading entire tree

questions.question: Explain the complete process of inserting a key-value pair into Ethereum's Merkle Patricia Tree, including how the tree maintains compression and how hashes are updated.
keyPoints: Convert key to hex nibbles for path traversal
Traverse existing nodes to find divergence point
Create new branch/leaf/extension nodes as needed
Apply compression rules for Extension nodes to maintain path compression
Recalculate hashes bottom-up from changed nodes to root
Update root hash after every state change

questions.question: Compare and contrast Patricia trees, tries, and Merkle trees in the context of blockchain technology. Explain why the Merkle Patricia Tree was chosen for Ethereum.
keyPoints: Tries: Simple prefix tree, one character per node, high memory usage
Patricia Trees: Compressed tries, variable-length edges, efficient space usage, no cryptographic verification
Merkle Trees: Binary tree with cryptographic hashing, supports verification but not efficient updates
MPT combines: Patricia structure for efficiency + Merkle hashing for security
Ethereum needs: Frequent state updates, proofs for SPV clients, efficient lookups - MPT provides all
